credit.name: household consumption protection credit
credit.amount_formula: 5 percent times a protected taxable-consumption allowance of $12,000 per adult and $6,000 per qualifying dependent
credit.indexing: CPI-U, rounded to the nearest $10, no nominal decrease
credit.income_measure: modified adjusted gross income using the joint-score legislative specification
credit.full_credit_through: 200 percent of the applicable HHS poverty guideline for household size
credit.phaseout: linear from 200 percent to 400 percent of the applicable HHS poverty guideline; zero at or above 400 percent
credit.filing_unit: federal income-tax unit with household-size poverty guideline determined from eligible adults and qualifying dependents
credit.eligible_person: U.S. citizen, U.S. national, or lawfully present resident treated as a U.S. resident for federal tax purposes with a valid SSN or ITIN; nonresident aliens excluded
credit.payment: monthly advance payment of one-twelfth through Treasury using tax, Social Security, and participating benefit-program records, with a nonfiler enrollment path
credit.reconciliation: annual reconciliation may increase an underpayment; no clawback for income-estimate error or ordinary household change absent fraud or material misrepresentation
credit.benefit_treatment: excluded from income and resources for federal means-tested benefits
credit.assignment_garnishment: not assignable and protected from garnishment except final adjudicated fraud recovery with due process

administration.lead: Department of the Treasury and Internal Revenue Service
data_partners: participating federal and state benefit agencies
administration.startup_period: enact no later than 2025-10-01 for a 2027-01-01 launch, subject to scorer and administrator feasibility review
requirements: multilingual paper, phone, in-person, and digital nonfiler enrollment
monthly payment status and correction portal
public payment timeliness, error, underpayment, and access reporting

score_requirements: household net incidence by income, age and family type
interaction with state sales taxes and benefits
